title: Does Cuba Really Have Iranian Drones?
gist: National security and Latin America experts tell Foreign Policy that Trump's unverified claims about Iranian drones stockpiled in Cuba are most likely being used to build a pretext for U.S. military action aimed at toppling the Havana government, with timing pressure from the approaching midterms and momentum from the recent Venezuela operation.

insights.claim: Foreign Policy could not independently verify whether military drones are present in Cuba; Trump said the U.S. is 'looking into' whether Cuba is stockpiling Iranian drones and suggested Washington could 'take care of it in short order' if confirmed.
insights.speaker: John Haltiwanger
insights.so_what: The underlying factual claim driving potential U.S. military escalation against Cuba remains unconfirmed — a key uncertainty for anyone pricing Caribbean geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: If they do have that, and they might very well have that … we'll take care of it in short order
insights.locator: "If they do have that, and they might very well have that

insights.claim: National security and Latin America experts told FP it's probable Cuba does possess some Iranian drones (likely a few hundred, such as Shahed-type UAVs), but they consider it extraordinarily unlikely that Havana plans any offensive action against the U.S., and see the recent alarm as part of a broader Trump administration effort to build a pretext for military action aimed at collapsing the Cuban government.
insights.speaker: John Haltiwanger
insights.so_what: The expert consensus separates two distinct claims — drone possession (plausible) versus offensive intent (implausible) — a distinction useful for filtering escalation rhetoric from actual risk.
insights.locator: The experts also believe that the recent alarm raised over drones

insights.claim: Brian Fonseca of Florida International University said Iran has 'proliferated cheap Iranian drone technology wherever they can' and Cuba is a 'potential market,' but called it 'crazy' to believe Cuba would plan preemptive action against the U.S., saying it 'would be suicidal for the regime.'
insights.speaker: Brian Fonseca
insights.so_what: A named regional-security expert directly rules out the offensive-threat rationale used to justify escalation, undercutting the administration's stated pretext.
insights.quote: It would be suicidal for the regime, even against the backdrop of what's going on now.
insights.locator: he added that it's "crazy" to believe

insights.claim: Fulton Armstrong, a former U.S. national intelligence officer for Latin America with 30-plus years of experience, said he repeatedly witnessed officials beat the drums on a 'threat that doesn't exist' in Cuba to make political gains, calling it 'a game for decades' used to justify aggressive action, and noted there has been no evidence of Cuban offensive intentions against the U.S., southern Florida, or Guantanamo in the 63 years since the Cuban missile crisis.
insights.speaker: Fulton Armstrong
insights.so_what: A former senior intelligence official with direct institutional memory frames the current drone alarm as part of a recurring, decades-long U.S. pattern rather than a novel threat — a strong historical base rate for discounting current claims.
insights.quote: People do this because you keep the relationship off balance and you give governments, including Democratic administrations, a pretext for doing aggressive things.
insights.locator: "People do this because you keep the relationship off balance

themes.theme: The broader pressure campaign against Havana

insights.claim: Trump's comments on Cuban drones came as the U.S. maintains a crippling oil blockade on Cuba — part of an apparent effort to topple the government and a dramatic escalation of the decades-long embargo — with Cuba just having experienced its third nationwide blackout in two weeks.
insights.speaker: John Haltiwanger
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence the U.S. is already applying severe economic pressure on Cuba independent of the drone narrative, indicating regime-change intent predates and doesn't depend on the drone claims.
insights.locator: Trump's comments on Monday came as the United States

insights.claim: Pentagon officials have reportedly been developing options for strikes on Cuba in recent weeks, according to CBS News, and Trump said in March, 'I do believe I [will be] having the honor of taking Cuba.'
insights.speaker: John Haltiwanger
insights.so_what: Active military planning against Cuba is reportedly underway, elevating the drone narrative from rhetoric to a live operational-planning context.
insights.quote: I do believe I [will be] having the honor of taking Cuba.
insights.locator: Trump has also discussed the possibility of using force

insights.claim: A 'constellation' of coordinated developments — Axios's May report citing classified intelligence on ~300 Cuban drones, CIA Director John Ratcliffe's visit to Havana, and the Justice Department's unsealing of indictments against former Cuban President Raúl Castro — points to a deliberate U.S. effort to lay the military-option groundwork, according to Brian Fonseca.
insights.speaker: Brian Fonseca
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, dated sequence of coordinated actions worth tracking as a template for how the administration builds intervention cases — useful for anticipating similar campaigns elsewhere.
insights.locator: He pointed to a "constellation" of developments

insights.claim: Secretary of State Marco Rubio, considered the architect of the administration's Cuba policy, called the country a 'national security threat' and accused it of being 'one of the leading sponsors of terrorism in the entire region'; the U.S. also imposed new sanctions this week targeting the Cuban government's funding sources.
insights.speaker: John Haltiwanger
insights.so_what: Rubio's personal role as policy architect (he is Cuban American) signals the Cuba pressure campaign has high-level, personally invested backing likely to persist and intensify.
insights.quote: one of the leading sponsors of terrorism in the entire region

themes.theme: Why a Cuba operation looks feasible and timely to the administration

insights.claim: Benjamin Gedan of the Stimson Center said it's 'silly to pretend that Cuba has a meaningful offensive military capability' given its small size and degraded military, and that even 300 drones would be 'quite inconsequential' to U.S. defenses — the drone narrative is 'just part of the case that's being made that Cuba is a bad actor.'
insights.speaker: Benjamin Gedan
insights.so_what: A direct expert rebuttal quantifying the actual military insignificance of the claimed threat, reinforcing that the drone story functions as narrative rather than genuine defense concern.
insights.quote: It's silly to pretend that Cuba has a meaningful offensive military capability.
insights.locator: "It's silly to pretend that Cuba has a meaningful offensive military capability

insights.claim: Fonseca said action against Cuba 'has to be this year' because if Republicans lose their House and Senate majorities in the November midterms, they are likely to be 'more unwilling to give the Trump administration unfettered support,' creating a narrow political window for any military move.
insights.speaker: Brian Fonseca
insights.so_what: Gives investors and risk analysts a concrete near-term timing window (before the November 2026 midterms) for potential U.S. military action against Cuba.
insights.locator: "I think it has to be this year," Fonseca said

insights.claim: Gedan said Trump 'seems persuaded that Cuba would be easy like Venezuela was and not a quagmire like Iran has become,' referencing the prior capture of former Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro during a U.S. military raid in January.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Gedan
insights.so_what: Frames Cuba as the administration's next 'easy target' after the successful Venezuela operation, distinct from the costlier Iran conflict — relevant precedent for anticipating operational approach and expected resistance level.
insights.quote: He seems persuaded that Cuba would be easy like Venezuela was and not a quagmire like Iran has become.
insights.locator: "He seems persuaded that Cuba would be easy like Venezuela was

insights.claim: There are credible, separate concerns about Cuba unrelated to the drone narrative — the reported presence of Russian and Chinese signals-intelligence gathering sites on the island — though even Gedan says it would be a real uphill battle to persuade the American public that the U.S. mainland (as opposed to Guantanamo) is at risk from Cuban drones.
insights.speaker: Benjamin Gedan
insights.so_what: Identifies the actually credible strategic concern (great-power intelligence infrastructure on Cuba) as distinct from the drone pretext, useful for separating real from manufactured justifications for policy.
insights.locator: This is not to say there aren't credible concerns
